steps.title: Pulmonology
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Tertiary Care

steps.title: Asthma
steps.text: What is the recommended initial oxygen delivery method for a child with severe or life-threatening asthma at tertiary level?
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends administering oxygen at 5 L/min via a face mask to ensure adequate oxygenation in severe asthma.

steps.title: Asthma
steps.text: What is the next step after administering 3 back-to-back salbutamol nebulisers in a child with life-threatening asthma?
steps.rationale: If there is a good response after 3 consecutive salbutamol nebulisations, the frequency can be reduced to hourly.

textChoices.text: If good response, stretch nebulisers to hourly

steps.title: Asthma
steps.text: Which of the following steroid regimens is appropriate for a child with life-threatening asthma?
steps.rationale: Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg STAT (up to a max of 10 mg) is one of the recommended steroid options. Hydrocortisone dose varies by age, and the other options are not specified in the guideline.

textChoices.text: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV for all ages

steps.title: Asthma
steps.text: What is an important component of fluid management for a child with severe life-threatening asthma at tertiary level?
steps.rationale: The guideline is to give 2/3 maintenance IV fluids with 20 mmol KCl per litre of fluid to maintain hydration and correct electrolyte imbalance.

textChoices.text: 2/3 maintenance IV fluids plus 20 mmol KCl per litre
Full maintenance fluids with no electrolytes

steps.title: Asthma
steps.text: How should IV magnesium sulphate be administered for a child with life-threatening asthma?
steps.rationale: Magnesium sulphate is diluted to a 10% solution and administered slowly at 0.4 ml/kg over 20 minutes to prevent side effects like hypotension.

textChoices.text: 0.4 ml/kg of 10% solution IV over 20 minutes

steps.title: Asthma
steps.text: When is ICU admission indicated in a child with life-threatening asthma?
steps.rationale: ICU admission is necessary if the child shows signs of impending respiratory failure (e.g., pulse >180/min, headache, convulsion)such as extreme tachycardia, convulsions, or other systemic symptoms.

steps.title: Laryngomalacia
steps.text: What percentage of laryngomalacia cases typically resolve by 2 years of age?
steps.rationale: Approximately 90% of laryngomalacia cases resolve spontaneously by the age of 2, so reassurance of parents is a key part of tertiary-level care.

steps.title: Laryngomalacia
steps.text: Which of the following therapies is recommended for associated gastroesophageal reflux disease in laryngomalacia?
steps.rationale: Acid suppression therapy, including proton pump inhibitors (e.g., omeprazole) or H2 blockers (e.g., famotidine), is recommended to manage reflux associated with laryngomalacia.

textChoices.text: Proton pump inhibitors or histamine receptor blockers

steps.title: Laryngomalacia
steps.text: What is the surgical intervention considered in severe and persistent cases of laryngomalacia?
steps.rationale: Supraglottoplasty is the surgical procedure reserved for severe and persistent laryngomalacia cases and typically involves ENT or pediatric surgical consultation.

steps.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
steps.text: What is a common surgical treatment for obstructive sleep apnea caused by adenotonsillar hypertrophy?
steps.rationale: Adenotonsillectomy is the first-line surgical intervention for OSA due to adenotonsillar hypertrophy.

steps.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
steps.text: When should a maxillofacial surgeon be consulted in a child with obstructive sleep apnea?
steps.rationale: Maxillofacial surgeons are consulted when structural facial issues are contributing to airway obstruction in OSA.

textChoices.text: When there are structural facial abnormalities
When the child fails positional therapy
When obesity is the sole contributing factor

steps.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
steps.text: Which of the following therapies is commonly used during sleep to manage obstructive sleep apnea?
steps.rationale: CPAP is the mainstay non-invasive treatment for OSA and works by keeping the upper airway open during sleep.

steps.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
steps.text: What positional advice should be given to a child with obstructive sleep apnea?
steps.rationale: Sleeping in a lateral position helps prevent airway collapse, which is more common in the supine position.

textChoices.text: Sleep in supine position for better breathing
Alternate between left and right lateral positions
Sleep in a lateral position instead of supine

steps.title: Bronchiectasis
steps.text: What is the recommended approach while awaiting sputum results in an acute exacerbation of bronchiectasis?
steps.rationale: Empiric antibiotics should be started promptly while awaiting sputum results to manage infection and reduce exacerbation severity.

textChoices.text: Delay antibiotics until culture is available

steps.title: Bronchiectasis
steps.text: Which of the following therapies is aimed at optimizing airway clearance in bronchiectasis?
steps.rationale: Chest physiotherapy is key in both acute and long-term management to enhance mucus clearance from airways.

steps.title: Bronchiectasis
steps.text: What mucolytic therapy may be used in bronchiectasis to assist with sputum clearance?
steps.rationale: Hypertonic saline nebulisers help thin and loosen mucus, facilitating its clearance from the airways.

steps.title: Bronchiectasis
steps.text: What prophylactic antibiotic is recommended for immunosuppressed bronchiectasis patients?
steps.rationale: Cotrimoxazole is used as a prophylactic antibiotic in immunosuppressed individuals to prevent infections.

steps.title: Bronchiectasis
steps.text: What therapy is part of growth and development management in children with bronchiectasis and cystic fibrosis?
steps.rationale: Children with cystic fibrosis often require pancreatic enzyme replacement to support nutrition and growth.

steps.title: Bronchiectasis
steps.text: What is the dosing regimen for azithromycin as an immune modulator in bronchiectasis?
steps.rationale: Azithromycin is given at 10 mg/kg three times per week for 3 months initially; continued if effective.

textChoices.text: 5 mg/kg 3 times a week for 1 month
10 mg/kg 3 times a week for 3 months

steps.title: Bronchiectasis
steps.text: When are inhaled corticosteroids recommended in the management of bronchiectasis?
steps.rationale: Inhaled corticosteroids are considered if wheezing is present, to reduce airway inflammation and improve symptoms.

textChoices.text: In all patients If the patient has wheezing
